title: Malted milk biscuits
subtitle: The crisp, malty British biscuit, made at home
excerpt: The homemade version of the classic malted milk biscuit: crisp, malty, with a faint sweetness and a dry snap. Malted milk powder gives the characteristic flavour; golden syrup contributes a little colour and the slight stickiness that holds the dough together.
sourceNotes: The malted milk biscuit was introduced commercially in Britain in 1924 by Peek Freans. The flavour combination of malt extract, dried milk, and wheat flour is documented in British biscuit-making literature of the twentieth century. This recipe synthesises the home-baking equivalent from the established tradition.

recipe.batchNotes: Double the recipe and bake in two batches.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in an airtight tin for 10 days. The malty flavour develops further after a day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan: 160 degrees C; the biscuits should be just golden but not darkened

glossaryTerms.definition: Malted milk powder is a blend of dried malt extract (from malted barley), dried whole milk, and wheat flour, processed to a fine powder. It is sold as a drink ingredient but also used in biscuit and confectionery baking to add a warm, slightly sweet, faintly caramel malt flavour. It contributes both flavour and a small amount of structure via the dried milk proteins. The proportion used in biscuits is low; the flavour is subtle rather than assertive.

content.text: The defining flavour in these biscuits is

, combined with a small amount of golden syrup for colour and flavour. The dough needs chilling before rolling; it is stiff and slightly sticky from the syrup. The biscuits should emerge from the oven looking barely coloured; they firm and darken slightly as they cool.

Combine the flour, malted milk powder, caster sugar, bicarbonate of soda, and salt in a large bowl. Rub in the cold butter until the mixture resembles fine breadcrumbs. Add the golden syrup and cold milk and mix to a firm dough; it will be stiff. Press into a flat disc, wrap in cling film, and refrigerate for 30 minutes. Preheat the oven to 180 degrees C (conventional). Line two baking trays with baking paper. Roll the cold dough to about 3 mm thick on a lightly floured surface and cut into rounds or rectangles with a 5 to 6 cm cutter. Transfer to the trays and prick each biscuit all over with a fork. Bake for 12 to 14 minutes until just golden at the edges. Leave on the trays for 5 minutes to firm before transferring to a rack.
